speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Ione Adair and Bernadine Cornelison took place at their home in Moscow on January 27th 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I think it said lavish garden parties and social gatherings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What are they talking about?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Over at the mansion, the museum, you'll find two photographs and those photographs are pictures of a garden party that we had down in underneath here. At that time this was a low plot in here and a high sidewalk went along that side and the garden party was put in here and we put a long table just outside the basement door and from that we served the people. There were, oh it must have been somewhere near a hundred of the people that were there and the picture shows the picture of the people sitting around in the garden in the high sidewalk. We wanted decorations for flowers for it and we had no idea where to get them and sister and I got in in the carriage and drove down towards Huylen and along the banks of this little stream along by between here and Huylen we saw bouquets flowers in bloom. We got out and got them. Nowadays you wouldn't think of getting them but they were great big heads of yellow flowers of some kind. Do you remember what tanzi looked like when they said, well the flower was somewhat like tanzi, but we gathered these great big arms full of these flowers and brought them back and put them in umbrella stands and garden ears around the different places in the yard. So we had decorations anyway.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it our garden or our beds in bloom?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Oh yes, mother had a big round bed down here that she had cannas in, cannas and geraniums and I was teaching at the Hwittwits school at that time which was the old school that was down where the where that new class building has been put on down there and I took the geraniums down and kept them in the school windows during this winter and mother put them out in the spring and around their canas in the flower bed. My father was very interested in snap dragons and he had a long bed, oh it was probably 10 -15 feet long, along the edge of the sidewalk that came from the upper yard down across and that was his snap dragon bed and anything happened to those snap dragons you were in for a marble mess. Well I was fascinated with a little gravel path. That was the old garden we did have. We had a couple of those big parties in there and many of the people that attended were the wives of professors and teachers themselves at the university that mother knew as I told you at one time that the people on one side of town had a certain day for calling and the people on this side of town had another day for calling and you called on those days and you always left the calling card to show that you'd been there. That little brass table up here is the calling card table that belonged to the wife of the head of the engineering department of Mr. Little at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well does that mean that the people from this side of town went over to the other side of town on that day and from the other side of town did their calling?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: So it would be one side of town calling on the other side of town. One had Wednesday and the other had either Tuesday or Friday or some one day they each side of town chose the day that they would be at home.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: So that side of town was the university professors and wives and this side were the towns, the business men and their wives.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: This was the business in the residence section for this side of town. Well that seems like quite a division in the town in those days. Really it was like two different towns then. They crossed back and forth then. They don't know. We don't know any of the people up there now but at that time the school was smaller and there were fewer people and teachers and professors in it than there are now and they mingle with the people on this side of town. They were a part of the town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What if a woman on this side of town wanted to visit your mother on that day?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Well I suppose they'd have to call and ask whether it was permissible to come over on that day or not. Of course probably not. But they had certain days by which they would be at home and everyone on that side stayed on their side of town for a callers on a certain day.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I don't tell him how what mother served at one of those afternoon teething. How mother served.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Honey? Do you remember what mother served one time? Did she tell you?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No. I liked it though.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: But we served with something special. They had just brought in canned pineapple. That was something most unusual. Most people were not. Until that time people didn't have anything like that. Pineapple was something which they didn't know a thing about. When they set their table outside this basement door down here, they put their long table and it all decorated and fixed up pretty. When they stepped out and brought them out on each little serving dish was a slice of lettuce and a slice of pineapple. On top of that a slice of banana and salad dressing on the top of that. That was their dessert for that day. Pineapple. No one had had pineapple before. No one they were eating.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really? It was the first time they'd ever had it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: First time we'd ever had pineapple.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Then the first time we ever saw pineapple my father was always eager to get something different. And so one day he came home with this wild looking thing and we didn't know what it was. It was a fresh pineapple and we didn't know how to attack it because it has all those swords and leaves and things. So I think Miss Sweet knew how to approach it with a butcher knife.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Miss Sweet was a librarian and she was living in the southwest corner bedroom upstairs. She came down to see if she could tell us what to do with pineapple. She had seen it served somewhere. I don't know where but she said you had to slice it down this way and cut down deep enough to get under the...

speaker: Ione Adair
words: peeling out of the pan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What is that little part?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It's a a cord that goes in. And you have to slice it down that way and then turn it over and cut it in small slices the other way. And that's the way you served your pineapple. Well we've learned long since and much easier ways of doing pineapple than that. But we did the pineapple and we got the pineapple and we served the pineapple later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well I want to ask you what was the purpose of having such a large gathering all at one time like that? Was it a special occasion?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: No special occasion excepting that mother was indebted to lots of these people on the other side and lots of people in town and this is Hannah that lived in the Schultz House just across the driveway on that slate colored house up here. Was also indebted to the people so they decided that they would go together and would serve an afternoon tea in the garden.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It was pretty cool. So that's how these were. Star shape and crescent shape and a little gravel path that went down around to the different... that path made a little path. So it was really a garden, a real... Yes this whole thing was a large garden.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: It's hard to believe now that one time there was a double row of trees along this side between what would be the building now and the street. There was a double row of box elder trees along there. There was a row across on the north and there were two large walnut trees out there which were a part of that at that time. And on the south side of the house was another double row of box elder trees. So the place was very shaded and very cool in here during the summertime. That's where my father had his snapdragons. My mother had her cannon, her anion belt.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you spend much time out of doors in the summer? Did you spend much time when you were here out of doors?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: When we were at home we spent a great deal of time around in the yard.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I always had sweet peas, I took a long roll of sweet peas. But I only took care of the roses. She has since she was a child. I say you took care of the rose beds, you loved the roses.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I had a rose bed, I think now that they have dug up the south, the upper portion of the south yard.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And the back, what is the back yard?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: I think Mr. Karmic had a, built a big garden in through there and has all kinds of plants in it and then back across. Well I hired a man from Genesee to come over and plot the ground for me. And my father bought rose bushes and put on the upper side next to the street. And I bought tulips and daffodils and put them along the edge of the wall. And the gardener brought in from the woods wild bleeding hearts, wild ferns, and other small plants that he could get. We had a wall that was possibly close to two feet high at the edge of the rose bed as it came into the under part of the garden.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Daddy got us a swing, one of those that is up on standards and two seats and swing back before that was on the back.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: And around that maple tree that's out there now, the gardener built a little fence or a seat around the maple tree and about the center of the garden outside. I had a birdbath and it was a willard pottery. If you know the willard pottery, I don't know whether you know anything about well or probably not. But the birdbath stood high and on top of the birdbath was a statue of a little goose boy with a goose in his arms. You've seen pictures of him, I'm sure. Well that was my birdbath out there. My mother every night made it a special trip down into the garden to pick up the goose boy and bring him up on the back porch for fear someone would walk off with him in the night. We still have a goose boy. I still have the goose boy and the bottom part, the standard of the birdbath. But our neighbor had a great big massive dog and he got thirsty one night and came down and raised up on the edge of it, knocked the goose boy off and also the birdbath and broke the birdbath into many many pieces.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have many birds using it?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes we did. We had, we've always watched an old woodpecker ever since we've been where in that house and since we moved over here. He's always had a nest in this first property tree out here, the tall one that stood up by itself the last one. He always nested in that tree and you'd hear him calling every morning bright and early and hear him giving a call to the other woodpeckers around. There was one woodpecker that had a nest in the, what was the bell tower of the Lutheran church which is now the senior citizens building. He had a nest in the tower there and he used to call back and forth these two old woodpeckers.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Tell them about what chased the woodpecker off.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: The woodpecker came back early one spring and started his building up there. The robins came a little later and they started building but they built in the popular tree to the south of the row of trees. The woodpecker had the one to the north. The robins didn't like the woodpecker and so every time the woodpecker appeared out here the robins were tackling and they would just go for him and made it very uncomfortable for him. One day we heard quite a racket outside and we went out to look and the robins were chasing the woodpecker and the woodpecker flew off and he was gone for quite a little while and finally back he came and he had reinforcements. This time he had four or five woodpeckers with him and what they did to the robins you can tell you. It was hard. It just went for those robins. Well the robins finally decided that they would keep the lower tree and the woodpecker could have the upper tree if he wanted it and the woodpecker stayed.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: So we now have a big squirrel. It goes up and down the tree. You know I wanted to ask you about the porch. The porch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you use the porch very much? The front porch?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes. This little front porch. The front porch was much narrower then than it is now and we had room for a seat or a couple of chairs out on the porch that we used. At this end of the port was the what Mrs. McConnell called her birdhouse. It was enclosed and had a door that came in from the front porch into it and they could also go in through the bedroom window into this enclosed porch. It was glass and she had geraniums and other flowers and birds. She called it her birdhouse. She had birds right inside there. They had birds but I think it was a parrot but I'm not sure. Maybe I'm not sure. I'm not sure but she had birds or at least one bird that I knew of. She had a house there for the birds in an enclosed porch. Well when Dr. Church took the place over from the Jacksons when they moved to Colfax and he decided the porch wasn't wide enough. He sat out there. So he hired a carpenter to widen the porch and put it up to the width that it is now and had the steps come up as they do now which they didn't come up that way when we were there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did they come when you were there?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: They came up but it was straight old -fashioned steps and now it's an enclosed pretty nearly enclosed. It's so much so that you have to shovel the snow before you can get up and down the steps in the in the wintertime. Well what did you do with that space that Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: McConnell used for the birdhouse?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We just used it to put the chairs or things that we had or plants in it and just used it as a storage for plants and other things.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: But it was the end of this porch. Dr. Church had a fix. He had the carpenters duplicate the kind of fancy what do you call them supports the railing. He didn't hear you Bernadine. Dr. Church had this carpenter duplicate those.

words: What do you call them?

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: Things that come from the railing down to the porch.

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: Yeah I know what you're talking about. He had it all duplicated. They had to do special work on it but he had the railing duplicated the one that was on before so it matched the small porch to the to the south of it. And at that time there was a railing all the way around the upstairs porch the top of the porch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Tall railing of the same same what do I mean?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Style porch. A railing. Well they duplicated the ones down below. Well it just came out the bedroom. There was a little one end to the south.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Really you would sleep on the upper porch?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes we used to sleep out there. Worried mother but we loved it. It was just like an album. I was always scared to death if you were I'd get up and walk off that porch some night. But it had this railing around. It did have a railing. I stayed up there. We put our blankets outside there and sleep in the summertime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Be nice and cool?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes although we were usually out of by boba in the summer but yes it was a lot to get out of. Oh that's nice. That must have been really nice. That was pretty good but we enjoyed it and we slip out there whenever it got hot in the house. We've never been suffering from the heat in the house because the ceilings were so tall. It's a matter of years. The house just never got hot. If you'd open the front doors. See there's a front door to that little porch and the front door to this porch on this side and a door on the other side and you open up all the windows and your house will cool off very quickly.

It had leaves with that large just velvety big green leaves and then the flowers came out on a long stem with little flowers kind of like a locust blossom. And my favorite pastime when I was real small was to get one of those leaves and make a hat and trim them with the different flowers out of the garden.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: But I always look forward to those catalpa trees. If you know a catalpa tree, you know what it is. The snapdragon, not the snapdragon, the bakshka blossoms, you know the bakshka blossoms. Well, they're all about that long. The catalpas are at least that long and they were spotted inside like a bakshka and you put one inside of the other and you could make a wreath out of them. Out of those Bernadine and Marjorie used to make wreaths and hats out of the catalpa tree. And the little maple tree that is south of the house now is a large tree, a very large tree. But when we had old bird bear, our little pet bear, he got loose one night and climbed the tree. My father wasn't good at climbing and he couldn't figure out how to get that bear down out of the tree. Chain caught over on the branch. He went way up into the top and then he got his chain caught on the branches. And dad knew he never could get up that high to get it. So what did he do but call the firemen and the fire brigade came up and the men went up the tree and cut the bear loose and brought him down. So then you know but that was a very small tree at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did your folks look at the house as a very special place when they lived in it? Did our people?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Yes, did they think of it as a very... We thought it as a home, nothing historical or anything like that because we knew that my father had turned his timber claim over to the bank in order to take possession. Mother wanted more room and mother had so much room then that she couldn't use all of it. And so if anyone wanted a room she very graciously left a room upstairs. We had Dr. Cogswill of the University Music Department. He was the first and Miss Sweet who was one of the first librarians also had a room upstairs and Dr. Gurney took the room after Miss Sweet left. And then we had then another room upstairs was rented out to Pearson, the man that made the bargain between my father and the bank for the transfer of the timber claim for the house. And he wanted that he said he wouldn't bargain with my father unless my father would promise him that he could have a room in the house that he was staying at the hotel and he didn't like hotel life. And he wanted a room where he would have a private room of his own and so forth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Bernadine when you say that your mother was particular what were her standards for these?

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: Well if someone came along like Professor Cogswill or Larry Gurney or Miss Sweet that she liked they would come and ask her and if she liked them she'd take them but she just doesn't think somebody came along. They had to have some special position in the background.

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: This old music professor Sir Isaac Cogswill was quite a character. He loved to give teas and he had a little plate upstairs then he had heat tea water on and he always would buy some little cookies or cakes downstairs downtown and serve tea to anyone that came in. And many a Sunday evening he'd come down and say I have cakes and tea come on up. We'd all go up and sit around on the floor in Dr. Cogswill's room and drink tea and eat boc cookies.

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: He composed a piece and dedicated it to me too I remember now.

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: I forgot you remember the piece. No he was quite a character.

speaker: Bernadine Cornelison
words: That was too tiny.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We never thought about it being anything unusual as far as the house was concerned. It was the largest house in town however. With the exception of the old Llewellyn or Woodwyth house down here now that house and this house were the largest houses that were in town and we never objected to mother having rumors because we always got the choice of anyone that wouldn't Tom that wanted rooms. They were all interesting. When he had this music professor he had his big piano.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He had a big Steinway, the Parlour Grand Steinway.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: He took it up the stairway and put it in his room. He had this in his room up there and the other was Dr. Gurney in the physics department and he was with us for 10 years at any rate and Miss Sweet was with us for at least 10 years.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: At different times. But in other words there was great demand to have a room in the house. You could have rented a room easily.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: She could have rented it to anyone but she always got someone. And they were people we enjoyed and people that were bartering me were kind of youngsters.

speaker: Ione Adair
words: We learned a lot from them. From this piano instructor he created a great desire for music. Mr. Gurney was an avid Victoria player. He had all the operatic records. I heard all these other operas and things from his side when he was there. And Miss Sweet got me so interested in books that when I was in college I worked in the library one year which was very interesting.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well does that mean that that you would trace your interest in music to that?

speaker: Ione Adair
words: Probably their influence. My mother was very musical. She was a performer but she was very musical, very naturally musical. But I was brought up with this atmosphere of music and books from the house.

I couldn't really tell you that. I can tell you one because I started them all. I was about seven I guess or eight and very much of the believer in what was said to me. The doorbell rang and I was the kind of answer to the door and see the people got to the party. And this man, it was the day this was the circus in town. And this man came to the door this big brass spirit -like thing and he said I have a dandy lion killer which you'll be interested in mind when he just started with the library. I tore down to the party. Mother there's a man who wants to send us a dandy lion killer. I said dandy lion, I got a dandy lion killer. I was a topic of conversation for a while.

words: Well, the circus was, he was, I want the circus but see we had seen the circus and I thought he meant to kill lions but he meant to go on the ground and get the dandy lion. And another time a man came to the door, mother had taught us never to fuss around with the plated cakes or cookies or surge to take the most available one instead of pouring down through to get something underneath. So that was firmly implanted in my code of ethics. The man came with a box of pins, fancy pins. There's turtles and grand hoppers and all kinds of things. Nothing I really wanted was a turtle. I just love turtles but it was way down underneath so I didn't dare go down through that to get the turtle. I took the grass off of it but I've loved turtle service since I was 21. I've just had for years and years and years. He's a cute little turtle too. Oh yeah, very cute, really nice. I don't want a key.

And he started in as a gardener there raising vegetable gardens and Boise. And then from that on up he came up to this part of the country and then the center's governor and his. No honey you're settled on the right track. But they lived in Yann Hill, Oregon. And at that time it was Oregon was not settled. It was the early pioneers. And the Indians used to come back and forth through there. And they were always on a rampage when they went through. And McConnell's had one son at that time. And young Ben had a Negro or Indian Pline 8. And this he and this little Indian boy played together all the time. Well the little youngster knew that the Indians were coming through that part of the country on a rampage. So he stole Ben and took him off into the woods and hid him and stayed with him until after the Indians had gone through. And then he brought him out and brought him back to his mother. I hope that's very interesting. That was young Ben's experience with Indians in the beginning. And then from Yann Hill, McConnell went as a young man down into the Boise country. And he was when he first went in he was a gardener and raised vegetables for those young mining people that were in that part of the country.
